metadata.title: Claude, Torn Down.
metadata.description: Skill teardown: nbb — the NikBearBrown Teardown register cut. Feynman × MKBHD voice, LLM exercise beat, teardown palette. Self-demo: Phase 2 register rewrite.

beats.narration_text: Ciao, Liam. Liam, in for Bear. Today: nbb — the NikBearBrown Teardown cut. You give it a finished reel, it forks the beat sheet, rewrites every line in the Teardown register, adds a paste-ready LLM exercise beat, and swaps the outro for the NikBearBrown close. Same facts. Different voice. Completely different audience.

props.runningText: * fork beat sheet → rewrite register → add LLM exercise → swap outro...

beats.narration_text: The nbb skill has one job: take any reel, any lecture, any book's worth of content, and produce the NikBearBrown Teardown version. The anatomy is three files plus a palette. The nbb brand data file: register rules, voice env var, palette tokens, outro spec. The brand_variant script: scaffold only — creates the nbb- directory and marks every narration as a todo. And the teardown voice guide: the Feynman honesty principle plus the MKBHD design-critic lens, in one file.

props.title: Feynman × MKBHD. Same facts, Teardown voice.

beats.narration_text: The pipeline has five phases. One: scaffold — brand_variant.py creates the nbb- directory and writes a beat_sheet.nbb.json with every narration field flagged as a todo. No audio spend, no fabrication. Two: rewrite — you open the new beat sheet and rewrite every narration in the Teardown register. Take it apart, reveal the mechanism, judge the design choices. Three: insert the LLM exercise beat — a paste-ready prompt the viewer can run today in Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini. Four: swap the outro for the NikBearBrown close. Five: build with GATE P first, then ElevenLabs audio — the only voice for this brand.

props.title: 5 phases: scaffold → rewrite → LLM exercise → outro → build

beats.narration_text: Before we walk through the mechanism, let me show you Phase 2 — the register rewrite. I am going to take one canonical narration beat and rewrite it in the Teardown register. The source is plain explanatory prose. The Teardown version takes it apart — shows the machinery, names the tradeoffs, judges the design choices.

props.command: Rewrite one mechanism beat in Teardown register
output: Teardown: take it apart, name the tradeoffs, judge the design

beats.narration_text: Here is the canonical narration: 'When you want the same content for a different audience, the nbb skill forks the beat sheet and rewrites narration in the Teardown register. It adds an LLM exercise and swaps the outro.' Now the Teardown version: Here is what actually happens. brand_variant.py clones your beat sheet into an nbb- directory and marks every narration field as a todo. That is not a bug; it is a constraint. The rewrite is intentional — you examine every sentence through the Teardown lens. Take it apart. Show the mechanism. Judge the choices. The LLM exercise is a paste-ready prompt — not a summary. Something the viewer can run today in Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini and get a useful result. The video is a prologue to practice, not a replacement for it.

props.title: Phase 2 — Register rewrite
props.code:
SOURCE (claude-liam register):
"When you want the same content for a different audience,
the nbb skill forks the beat sheet and rewrites narration
in the Teardown register. It adds an LLM exercise
and swaps the outro."

TEARDOWN REWRITE (Feynman × MKBHD):
"Here is what actually happens. brand_variant.py clones
your beat sheet into an nbb- directory and marks every
narration field as a todo. That is not a bug; it is a
constraint. You examine every sentence through the
Teardown lens. Take it apart. Show the mechanism.
Judge the choices. The LLM exercise is a paste-ready
prompt — not a summary. Something the viewer can run
today in Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini and get a useful
result. The video is a prologue to practice."

beats.narration_text: The first mechanism: take it apart. The Teardown register has one rule above all others — explain how each piece actually works: the machinery, not just the name. Strip jargon. Reveal the mechanism. Every beat in the original reel was written to explain a concept to a general audience. The Teardown version explains the engineering decision behind that concept. Why was it built this way? What did that choice optimize for? What did it sacrifice? That is the question every Teardown narration answers.

props.heading: Take it apart. Show the machinery.
props.body: Every beat answers: why was this built this way? What did that choice optimize for? What did it sacrifice? Teardown register strips jargon and reveals the engineering decision behind each concept. Not just what it does — how it actually works and what that costs.
props.quote: Take it apart: explain how each piece actually works — the machinery, not just the name.

beats.narration_text: The second mechanism: change the voice, not the facts. This is the hard constraint the nbb skill enforces by design. The scaffold marks every narration as a todo — a forcing function. You can not just auto-translate. You have to read each beat, understand what fact it was communicating, and reconstruct that fact in the Teardown voice. No fabrication. No new claims. The Teardown version of a beat and the canonical version must agree on every factual statement. The voice is different; the facts are identical.

props.heading: Change the voice, not the facts.
props.body: The scaffold marks every narration as a todo — a forcing function against auto-translation. You read each beat, understand the fact, reconstruct it in Teardown voice. No fabrication. No new claims. Teardown and canonical must agree on every factual statement. Voice different; facts identical.
props.quote: Change the voice, not the facts.

beats.narration_text: The third mechanism: ElevenLabs only. The nbb skill has one non-negotiable rule about audio: the NikBearBrown ElevenLabs clone is the only voice. Not Kokoro. The reason is brand integrity — the Teardown register lives or dies on the voice. The Bear clone delivers the Feynman honesty and MKBHD confidence in a single take. Swapping it for a different voice breaks the brand contract. GATE P applies before any ElevenLabs spend — you verify the pedagogy before the clock starts on the metered clone.

props.heading: ElevenLabs only. GATE P first.
props.body: The Bear ElevenLabs clone is the only voice for nbb. Not Kokoro. Brand integrity requires it — the Teardown register lives on the voice. GATE P (PEDAGOGY.md VERDICT: PASS) must run before any audio spend. The metered clone runs on approval only.
props.quote: The NBB ElevenLabs voice is the only option — do not substitute Kokoro.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Torn Down. The nbb skill delivers four things: a forked nbb- directory with the canonical untouched, every narration rewritten in the Teardown register — Feynman honesty plus MKBHD design-critic lens, a paste-ready LLM exercise beat the viewer runs today, and the NikBearBrown outro. Gate P before the Bear clone runs. This is the default channel when the content demands the machinery be visible.

artifactLines: nbb- directory — canonical beat_sheet.json never touched
Teardown register rewrite: Feynman honesty + MKBHD design-critic lens
LLM exercise beat — paste-ready prompt + dig-deeper follow-up
NikBearBrown outro + GATE P before ElevenLabs spend

beats.narration_text: Two questions before you run nbb. First: have you read the Teardown voice guide — voices/teardown/VOICE.md — so your rewrites actually sound like Bear, not a neutralized summary? Second: is the canonical beat_sheet.json untouched? The scaffold creates the nbb- directory; if you edited the source, that is the wrong file.

output: 1. Read VOICE.md — rewrites must sound like Bear
